difficulty_factors: Lycopene (carotenoid dye) is extremely persistent — standard tannin spotters are ineffective against it
Three-phase stain: cooking oil, tomato acid/tannin, lycopene pigment
Lycopene is oil-soluble — dissolves in fat but not water, requiring solvent before oxidizer
Oxidizer (peroxide) works better than tannin spotter on carotenoid pigments
Linen is strong cellulose but wrinkles easily and can yellow with aggressive bleaching

diy.title: Tomato Sauce on Linen
ingredients: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (this is the key agent for lycopene)
mixSteps: Scrape off surface sauce first — do not rub
Mix 1 drop dish soap into ½ cup cool water
Have peroxide 3% and ammonia ready — the oxidizer is the critical agent for the red lycopene pigment

steps.instruction: Scrape off as much surface tomato sauce as possible with a spoon or dull knife. Work in one direction — don't smear. Then flush from the back with COLD water to push remaining sauce out through the fabric.
steps.why: Remove solids first. Tomato sauce contains cooking oil that spreads when liquid is applied. Cold water flushes water-soluble components (tomato acid, dissolved sugars) while the sauce is still fresh.

steps.instruction: Apply a drop of dish soap directly to the stain. Work in gently. Let sit 5 minutes. Rinse with cool water.
steps.why: Tomato sauce typically contains olive oil or another cooking oil. The surfactant emulsifies this oil component. If you skip this step, the oil creates a hydrophobic barrier that prevents the water-based oxidizer from reaching the lycopene pigment underneath.

steps.instruction: Dab white vinegar onto the stain. Let sit 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cool water.
steps.why: Vinegar addresses the tannin component — tomato contains natural tannin polyphenols in addition to the lycopene. The acid breaks tannin-fiber hydrogen bonds. This handles the orange-brown component but NOT the red lycopene.

steps.instruction: Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% generously to the remaining red/orange stain. Add 2–3 drops of clear ammonia. Let sit 5 minutes. Rinse with cool water. Apply vinegar. Rinse again.
steps.why: THIS IS THE KEY STEP. Lycopene is a carotenoid dye — a long conjugated carbon chain that absorbs light in the red/orange spectrum. Standard tannin spotters don't work on lycopene because it's not a polyphenol (tannin). Hydrogen peroxide oxidizes lycopene by breaking the conjugated double-bond chain, destroying the chromophore and eliminating the red color. Ammonia catalyzes the peroxide. Linen (cellulose fiber) tolerates this chemistry well.

steps.instruction: If red/orange persists, lay the damp fabric in direct sunlight for 30–60 minutes after peroxide treatment. Then wash with enzyme detergent in warm water.
steps.why: UV light from sunlight accelerates photobleaching of lycopene. Carotenoids are photosensitive — UV energy breaks the conjugated bond system. Sunlight + peroxide is dramatically more effective than either alone. Linen tolerates UV better than most fabrics.

steps.instruction: Wash in warm water with enzyme detergent. Air dry and inspect. Do NOT use dryer until stain is confirmed gone.
steps.why: Heat sets residual lycopene and any remaining tannin. Verify removal before applying heat.

diy.ifStillThere: Repeat the peroxide + sunlight treatment — lycopene often needs 2–3 rounds. For white linen: OxiClean soak (1 scoop per gallon warm water, 1–6 hours) provides extended oxidation. Last resort: dilute chlorine bleach (1:10) on white linen only — safe on cellulose but test colored linen for dye safety first.
proTips: Lycopene is NOT a tannin — tannin removers won't fix the red color. You need an OXIDIZER (peroxide).
Sunlight is your secret weapon. After peroxide treatment, 30 minutes of direct sun dramatically accelerates lycopene destruction.
The red color is lycopene (carotenoid). The orange-brown color is tomato tannin. Different chemistry for each.
If the sauce contained meat (Bolognese), treat as a protein combination — add enzyme step before oxidizer.
diy.safety: Test ammonia and peroxide on hidden seam. Linen is generally tolerant, but colored linens may lose dye. NEVER mix ammonia with chlorine bleach. Work in ventilated area.
diy.whyThisWorks: Tomato sauce is a three-phase stain: cooking oil, tomato tannin, and lycopene (carotenoid pigment). The oil is handled by surfactant, the tannin by acid, but the persistent red color is lycopene — a carotenoid dye that requires oxidation (peroxide) not acid (tannin spotter). UV light provides the final push by photobleaching the carotenoid chromophore.

home.title: Tomato Sauce on Linen — Confident Removal
home.confidenceNote: Linen tolerates strong chemistry. Lycopene responds well to peroxide + UV. Aged stains may need multiple peroxide rounds.

primaryProduct.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
primaryProduct.why: Extended oxygen bleach soak — the sustained peroxide generation is ideal for lycopene oxidation

alternativeProducts.name: Zout Triple Enzyme Stain Remover
alternativeProducts.why: If sauce contains meat — protease for protein, lipase for oil component
alternativeProducts.amazonSearchQuery: Zout triple enzyme stain remover spray laundry

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dishwashing Liquid
alternativeProducts.why: Surfactant for the cooking oil component — apply before OxiClean

home.prepStep: Scrape off sauce. Flush from back with cold water. Address oil FIRST before oxidizer.

method.instruction: Scrape off sauce. Flush from back with cold water.
method.why: Solid removal prevents spreading. Back-flush pushes stain out.

method.instruction: Apply Dawn dish soap to the oily residue. Work gently for 5 minutes. Rinse.
method.why: Surfactant clears the oil barrier so peroxide can reach the lycopene pigment.

method.instruction: Make OxiClean paste (powder + splash warm water). Apply to stain. Let sit 30 minutes. Rinse.
method.why: Sustained peroxide generation from percarbonate at alkaline pH — the key to oxidizing lycopene.

method.instruction: If red/orange persists: lay damp linen in direct sunlight for 30–60 minutes. Then wash warm.
method.why: UV light photobleaches lycopene. Sunlight + oxidizer is dramatically more effective than either alone.

materialWarnings.warning: Strong cellulose fiber — tolerates full chemistry including enzymes, peroxide, even dilute chlorine bleach on whites.
Wrinkles easily when wet — lay flat or iron damp for best results.
Test peroxide and OxiClean on hidden area — some linen dyes are not colorfast to oxygen bleach.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: Multiple rounds of peroxide + sunlight. If stain persists after 3 rounds on white linen, dilute chlorine bleach (1:10) as last resort.

proTips: Sunlight is free and highly effective on lycopene — use it.
Lycopene is oil-soluble — remove the oil phase FIRST or peroxide can't reach it.
Multiple short peroxide treatments beat one long soak. H₂O₂ loses effectiveness after 2–4 hours.
home.whyThisWorks: Lycopene's conjugated bond chain absorbs red light. Peroxide oxidizes those bonds, shortening the chain until it no longer absorbs visible light. UV light accelerates this by providing activation energy directly to the chromophore.

pro.title: Tomato Sauce on Linen — Professional Combination Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (oil + tannin + carotenoid dye). Three distinct chemical phases: (1) Cooking oil (olive oil, vegetable oil) — triglyceride requiring surfactant or POG. (2) Tomato tannin — polyphenolic compounds responding to acidic tannin formula. (3) Lycopene — a C₄₀ carotenoid with 11 conjugated double bonds that absorbs at 444–505nm (red/orange). Lycopene is NOT a tannin and does NOT respond to tannin spotters. Requires OXIDATION to break the conjugated chain. H₂O₂ cleaves double bonds through oxidative addition. UV light provides photolytic activation energy directly to the chromophore. Linen: cellulose fiber, pH 6.0–11.0, heat 90°C, chlorine bleach safe on whites, all enzymes safe. Strong, durable fiber.

spottingProtocol.technique: Spatula — remove solid/semi-solid sauce before chemistry
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape off all surface sauce. On linen, which is coarser than cotton, sauce tends to sit on the surface longer before penetrating — speed helps.

spottingProtocol.technique: Water gun from back of fabric
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water flush from back. Removes water-soluble components (tomato juice, sugars, citric acid).

spottingProtocol.technique: Address cooking oil phase — dry-side before wet-side
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG or concentrated surfactant (NSD) for the cooking oil component. Tomato sauce almost always contains olive oil, canola oil, or similar cooking fat. This oil creates a hydrophobic barrier that blocks water-based chemistry. Clear it first. Tamp with brush. Flush with VDS or steam.

spottingProtocol.technique: Acidic tannin spotter for tomato polyphenols
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply tannin formula for the polyphenol (tannin) component. Tamp with brush. This addresses the orange-brown component of the stain. Flush with water. Neutralize acid on cellulose with neutral lubricant or protein formula.

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidizing bleach — the CRITICAL step for lycopene destruction
spottingProtocol.instruction: THIS IS THE KEY STEP. Lycopene is a carotenoid — its red color comes from 11 conjugated C=C double bonds. Tannin formula can't touch it. Only oxidation breaks these bonds. Apply H₂O₂ 6% + 1 drop 28% ammonia per 8 oz. The perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻) performs oxidative addition across the conjugated chain, shortening it below visible-light absorption threshold. On linen: 6% peroxide is safe. Let work 5–10 minutes — lycopene takes longer to oxidize than simple tannins. Flush and neutralize with acetic acid.

spottingProtocol.agent: UV exposure (sunlight) — optional but highly effective
spottingProtocol.instruction: If orange/red persists after peroxide: apply fresh peroxide and expose to direct sunlight. UV photons provide the activation energy to directly cleave lycopene's conjugated bonds — this is photolytic decomposition. Lycopene is photosensitive (same reason tomatoes lose color in sunlight). Peroxide + UV is dramatically more effective than either alone. Linen tolerates UV exposure better than most fibers.

Acetic acid neutralizes ammonia alkalinity. Thorough flush. Per NEUTRALIZE_ACID_AFTER_TANNIN rule — but here we're neutralizing the alkali from the peroxide step. On cellulose, always ensure final rinse leaves fiber near-neutral.

pro.escalation: If lycopene persists after 2 rounds of peroxide + UV: (1) Stronger peroxide (12% with caution — test on linen first). (2) Sodium percarbonate extended soak (1–6 hours, warm water). (3) On white linen: sodium hypochlorite 1:10, 15 min max — effective on lycopene but tests dye safety first. (4) Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) — different mechanism, attacks chromophore by reduction rather than oxidation.
pro.customerExplanation: Tomato sauce leaves three different types of stain: cooking oil, plant tannin, and lycopene — the red pigment in tomatoes. The red color is the hardest part because lycopene is a specialized dye that doesn't respond to standard stain removers. We use hydrogen peroxide to chemically break the pigment's color-causing structure, and in stubborn cases, sunlight helps accelerate this process because lycopene is naturally sensitive to UV light.
commonMistakes: Using only tannin spotter and expecting the red to go away — lycopene is NOT a tannin
Skipping the oil removal step — cooking oil blocks peroxide from reaching the lycopene underneath
Using hot water first — sets any protein component (meat sauce) and drives oil deeper
Not using sunlight — the most effective free tool for lycopene destruction
Giving up after one round of peroxide — lycopene often requires 2–3 oxidation cycles
pro.neutralization: Acetic acid after peroxide/ammonia step. Neutral lubricant after tannin formula step. Full flush between chemistry changes. On linen, ensure thorough drying — linen is susceptible to mildew if stored damp.
pro.fabricRisks: Linen: cellulose fiber. pH 6.0–11.0. Heat to 90°C. All enzymes safe. All solvents safe. Chlorine bleach safe (whites only). Strong fiber — tolerates aggressive treatment. Wrinkles easily. Mildew-susceptible if stored damp.
